how-i-built-search: Built a self-updating site search with dropdown previews by extracting post metadata into a shared source file and writing a build script that auto-generates the search index—eliminating the brittleness of hand-maintained JSON. The key insight: ten minutes of upfront refactoring (centralizing the posts list) paid for itself by enabling automation that scales as the site grows, avoiding silent failures when new content gets added but forgotten in search.
how-i-automated-garmin-recaps: This writer built an automated system that pulls daily Garmin health data via Python API, generates AI-written recaps using Claude CLI, and emails them every morning at 7am using macOS launchd scheduling—taking ~2.5 hours across two sessions to prototype, debug, and deploy. The core insight: health data collection tools exist, but the friction of checking dashboards kills consistency; packaging the data as actionable morning emails removes that friction entirely.
how-i-replaced-google-forms: Built a custom contact form in 2 hours using Claude Code, Supabase, and Resend—replacing a generic Google Form with a branded component that stores submissions in a database and sends instant email notifications. The key win: using Claude Code's terminal-based file editing was 4x faster than browser-based workflows, cutting setup time from 8–10 hours (first Supabase project) to 2 hours by eliminating copy-paste friction and leveraging prior knowledge of the database layer.
how-i-built-greetings: Built a dynamic greeting system that rotates 50+ time-aware and day-aware messages on the homepage, replacing 8 static words with contextual variations (e.g., "coffee brewing" at 7am on Tuesday vs. "late-night building" at midnight). The key learning: ruthlessly trim first drafts (Claude generated 25–35 word greetings that needed cutting to 8–12), avoid packing all context into every variant, and iterate on copy more than code—6 rounds of refinement took 90 minutes but made the difference between over-engineered and actually human.
how-i-built-footer: Built a production-ready footer component for joseandgoose.com in 25 minutes using Claude Code (terminal-based AI with direct file access), 7x faster than the same workflow with Claude.ai in-browser. Key learning: eliminating manual file-switching friction between Claude and your editor dramatically accelerates design iteration, especially for self-contained components with no backend complexity.
how-i-built-numerator: A non-developer built Numerator, a live multiplayer game based on a game theory puzzle, in ~8–10 hours across 4 sessions using Claude as a pair programmer, Supabase for the database, and Vercel for hosting. The key lesson: locking down the game spec with Claude's clarifying questions upfront caught a critical bug (30% vs 50%) and saved hours of rework, proving that AI pair programming works best when you invest time in defining requirements before writing code.
gemini-grades: The author had Google's Gemini AI independently grade the difficulty of building joseandgoose.com to validate whether "beginner-friendly" was accurate—it confirmed the assessment, but revealed that while design and deployment are genuinely easy, environment setup and DNS configuration require patience and troubleshooting rather than coding knowledge. The real hurdle isn't technical skill but comfort with terminals, multiple platforms, and iterative fixes, plus a paid Claude subscription ($20/mo).
how-i-built-this: A non-developer built a fully deployed custom website in ~6–7 hours across three sessions using Claude to generate code, Next.js + Vercel for hosting, and free tools for analytics and DNS. The key insight: AI can now write complete, production-ready websites with mobile responsiveness and deployment config, compressing what normally costs $5k–$20k and requires hiring into an afternoon learning project. The main friction points were environment setup and debugging Terminal commands, but once the local dev server ran, iterating on design and features became straightforward prompt-and-paste cycles.
how-i-built-tldr: Built AI-generated TL;DR summaries for every post by generating them once at publish time via Claude Haiku, storing results in static JSON, and bundling with the build—eliminating runtime API calls, latency, and scaling costs. The architectural decision (build-time generation vs. real-time API calls) proved more valuable than the feature itself, and a silent environment variable failure early on showed the importance of defensive error handling in unattended scripts.
